nativeRange.habitat: The Angelfish is found in slow-moving rivers, flooded forests, and swampy areas with dense aquatic vegetation, and is commonly encountered in the still backwaters and tributaries of the Amazon basin.
nativeRange.biotope: Amazon basin — dense vegetation, leaf litter, dark tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: tall leafy plants (Amazon sword, anubias, vallisneria)
vertical surfaces for spawning (slate, broad-leaved plants)

diet.notes: Readily accepts quality flakes and pellets. The diet benefits from regular frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and krill. Occasional blanched vegetables — spinach or zucchini — can supplement the diet. Feeding two to three small meals daily is generally recommended over one large feeding.

goodWith: medium-sized tetras (black skirt, cardinal, rummy nose)
avoidWith: very small nano fish (will be eaten)
fast aggressive species that may outcompete

imageCandidates.notes: Versions111, 3020x3102. Tack-sharp, vibrant gold captive-bred variety showing the classic tall compressed body and long trailing ventral filaments; clean single subject against green background.
CONTERALLY, 3200x2400. Crisp full-body marble variety with dramatic black/white marbling and high veil finnage; printed skyline aquarium background but the fish itself is sharp and striking.
James St. John, 2107x1659. Wild-type silver form showing the diagnostic dark vertical bars and red eye, with additional individuals behind (shoaling context); slightly dim but head in sharp focus.

summary: The Angelfish is a tall-bodied cichlid from the Amazon basin, available in many captive-bred color and fin varieties. It requires a tall tank with at least 60 cm of vertical swim space and is generally considered an intermediate-level species. Pairs form monogamous bonds.
careNotes:
Tank height is critical: the tall compressed body requires at least 60 cm of vertical swim space — standard low-profile tanks are generally unsuitable. A minimum of 200 L tall-format tank is advised, especially for pairs or groups. Most trade specimens are captive-bred, commonly hybrids between P. scalare and related forms. The Altum Angelfish (P. altum) is a distinct and larger species (up to 25–30 cm tall) occasionally available at specialist retailers and should not be confused with the common aquarium angelfish.

Angels can be kept singly, in pairs, or in groups of 6+; keeping 2–5 fish tends to result in one dominant fish harassing the rest. When kept as a group, the largest aquarium feasible is recommended to allow subdominant fish to establish territories.

Spawning pairs become notably semi-aggressive, defending a chosen spawning site against all tankmates. Tiger Barbs, Serpae Tetras, and other confirmed fin-nippers should be strictly avoided — angels' long trailing fins are highly susceptible to nipping damage.

Adult body length is typically around 12–15 cm; total height including extended dorsal and ventral fins can reach approximately 20–25 cm.

Captive-bred individuals have often been acclimated to harder, more neutral water than wild-caught fish; most community-tank water conditions (pH 6.5–7.5, moderate hardness) are generally well tolerated.
breedingNotes: Angelfish are generally considered moderate in breeding difficulty. Pairs typically form naturally from a group of 6+ juveniles allowed to grow out together. Once a mated pair forms, they tend to bond monogamously. Spawning occurs on cleaned vertical surfaces — slate tiles, broad-leaved plants, or the aquarium glass. Clutch sizes of 100–300+ eggs are common; eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days at 28°C, and fry are free-swimming within another 3–5 days. Both parents tend to guard eggs and early fry vigorously (biparental care). Fry accept freshly hatched brine shrimp as a first food. Some pairs resort to eating their first few spawns before settling into reliable parental care; separating eggs to a hatching tank is an option but may impair future parental instinct.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary due to Aquarium Co-Op URL 404. SeriouslyFish provided tank dimensions, temperature, pH, hardness, size, diet, behavior, and breeding notes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (78–86°F / 25.6–30°C), pH (6.0–8.0), adult size (15 cm body / 20 cm total), lifespan (8–12 years), tankmate guidance, and breeding notes. Consistent with primary.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75–82°F / 24–28°C), pH (6.8–7.0), hardness (4–12 dGH), diet, lifespan (10–12 years). Reported adult body size (3–4 inches body, 6 inches height) is a lower outlier than other sources; other sources converge on 15 cm body. Minimum tank size quoted (20 gal / 76 L) is not representative of a proper long-term setup for this species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Cichlidae / Cichliformes), native range (Amazon basin: Brazil, Peru, Colombia, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana), temperature (24–30°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–13 dH). Wild max TL reported as 10.5–15 cm.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs typically form when a group of six or more juveniles is raised together and allowed to bond naturally. Angelfish are biparental substrate spawners, depositing eggs on vertical surfaces. Breeding is generally considered moderate in difficulty, primarily because of pair-bond dynamics and the tendency for early spawns to be eaten before reliable parental care is established.
